meta.title: Step-by-step glaze work instructions
meta.note: These procedures are practical studio instructions for testing and expanding glaze knowledge. They are written to slow down guesswork and make every batch more comparable.

procedures.name: Build a 1000 g test batch from a 100-part recipe
procedures.goal: Turn a recipe into a comparable test batch without introducing avoidable process noise.
steps: Choose one recipe and one body for the batch. Do not mix several goals into the same test.
Convert the recipe to a 1000 gram dry batch so each percentage equals 10 grams.
Weigh each material cleanly into a dry bucket, starting with the major materials and ending with small colorants or opacifiers.
Dry blend first so a later wet lump is not mistaken for a bad recipe.
Add water gradually instead of all at once, then mix thoroughly.
Sieve the glaze through an appropriate mesh and remix after sieving.
Label the bucket with recipe id, body, date, and intended firing.
checkpoints: all weights match the written batch sheet
the glaze is uniform after sieving
the batch label is complete before any tile is dipped
commonMistakes: changing the batch size and the recipe at the same time
adding the water too fast and then blaming the recipe for slurry problems
forgetting to label the batch until after several similar tests exist

procedures.name: Set bucket consistency before testing
procedures.goal: Make application behaviour consistent enough that thickness differences are intentional rather than accidental.
steps: Mix the glaze thoroughly after it has rested so the solids are fully back in suspension.
Check whether the glaze feels watery, creamy, or overly gelled before changing anything.
Adjust water in small increments only if the glaze is clearly too thick to apply evenly.
If the glaze settles hard or applies streaky, fix the process issue before changing chemistry.
Use flocculation or suspension aids only in small recorded amounts.
Apply a quick test dip on a scrap tile and let it dry so you can judge coat quality.
checkpoints: the glaze applies in a repeatable coat instead of a random flood
the dry layer does not dust off immediately or crack badly on drying
any process aid added to the bucket is recorded
commonMistakes: using a chemistry edit to solve a slurry problem
adding a process aid without writing down how much
judging consistency from one stir instead of from real application

procedures.name: Run a thickness ladder
procedures.goal: Find the first stable useful thickness zone instead of choosing a glaze from one arbitrary coat.
steps: Choose one test tile shape with room for several thickness bands.
Apply the first band thin, the middle band at your normal thickness, and the top band deliberately heavy.
Keep the clay body and firing identical across all thickness ladders in the same round.
Write the thickness order on the back or in the firing log so it cannot be forgotten later.
Fire all ladders together with witness cones.
Read the ladder from thinnest to thickest and mark the first zone that looks stable, mature, and useful.
checkpoints: the same glaze shows a visible progression instead of random application noise
the chosen production thickness is based on evidence, not on the prettiest accidental band
run distance or opacity change is recorded
commonMistakes: testing only one coat count on a reactive glaze
choosing the most dramatic run zone instead of the first stable usable zone
forgetting to compare the ladder on the actual production body

procedures.name: Choose a cone-6 clear host
procedures.goal: Pick the right clear before adding stains, underglaze, or reactive colour branches.
steps: Select one stable general clear, one low-expansion porcelain clear, and one warm-colour host clear.
Apply all three on the same body and form shapes so the comparison stays honest.
Fire a clear-only round first with no colour under or over the host.
Inspect rims, handles, and edges for crazing or shivering instead of judging from flat tiles alone.
Only after the clear host passes the fit check should you place stains, underglaze, or decals under it.
If warm colours are important, repeat the comparison with the actual warm-colour system before choosing the winner.
checkpoints: the chosen host fits the body
the chosen host stays clear at the intended thickness
sensitive warm colours were tested in the real host instead of assumed
commonMistakes: testing colour first and host second
choosing the clearest host on stoneware and assuming it will also fit porcelain
assuming every transparent is an equally good warm-colour host

procedures.name: Rebuild a material substitution deliberately
procedures.goal: Preserve the test logic when a key material has to change.
steps: Write down why the substitution is happening: supply, safety, fit, gassing, or colour.
Keep one control batch of the original recipe whenever the material is still available and safe to test.
Identify whether the old material carried chemistry, slurry behaviour, or both.
Build the first substitution branch with the minimum change needed to answer the question.
Fire the control and the new branch together on the same body and at the same thickness.
Only after the first branch is understood should you tune silica, clay, or another flux.
checkpoints: the substitution reason is clear in the log
the control and substitute are comparable
the fired difference can be tied to one intentional change
commonMistakes: making a substitution and a chemistry correction in the same first test
forgetting that a material can change bucket behaviour as much as fired chemistry
calling a pretty accident a successful substitute without checking the original intent

procedures.name: Develop a colour from a proven clear host
procedures.goal: Separate host choice from colour loading so the colour test teaches something real.
steps: Choose a clear host that already fits the body and looks right on its own.
Pick one colour family only, such as celadon, cobalt blue, or chrome-tin pink.
Create a short line blend of the colourant or stain in that one host.
Keep application thickness constant so the colour comparison is not secretly a thickness comparison.
If the family is sensitive, repeat the same colour line in one alternate host rather than changing the colour percentages again.
Promote the lowest loading that reaches the colour target without burying the host.
checkpoints: the host was proven first
only one colour family is being mapped at a time
the chosen colour load is the first successful zone, not the most extreme
commonMistakes: switching host and colourant amount in the same test round
using a reactive base when the goal was to understand the colour itself
adding more colourant to solve a weak host

procedures.name: Diagnose pinholes before editing the recipe
procedures.goal: Rule out firing and process problems before tearing apart a glaze that may be chemically fine.
steps: Check whether the glaze is being applied too thickly for the body and surface shape.
Review the bisque and glaze firing for burn-out speed, top soak, and actual witness-cone result.
Look for high-LOI materials, organics, or contamination in the body and glaze stack.
Fire one process-adjusted batch before making a chemistry edit.
If the pinholes remain, then compare the original recipe against a lower-gas or more open-melt branch.
Record whether the problem changed during firing, during cooling, or only after over-thick application.
checkpoints: the schedule was checked before chemistry was blamed
thickness and body preparation were reviewed
the chemistry edit is based on a remaining problem, not on guesswork
commonMistakes: adding frit or removing carbonate before checking the firing itself
reading blistering, crawling, and pinholing as the same defect
judging the whole recipe from one bad test tile

procedures.name: Convert a reactive prototype into a usable glaze
procedures.goal: Move from a dramatic test result toward something a studio can actually repeat on ware.
steps: Start with the most successful reactive test and define what part of it is non-negotiable: colour, break, run, or texture.
Run a thickness ladder to find the first stable usable zone.
Test the glaze on the real ware shape, not only on a dramatic vertical tile.
If it still runs too much, move one step toward a more stable host or a slightly stiffer chemistry branch.
Retest the same colour family after the stability change instead of assuming it survived unchanged.
Promote the version that keeps the core effect at the lowest operational risk.
checkpoints: the studio knows what part of the original effect actually mattered
the chosen version works on the intended form
stability was improved without losing all of the visual reason for the glaze
commonMistakes: trying to preserve every dramatic feature of a test-only glaze
fixing run risk without rechecking colour or break
judging usability from catch-plate drama alone

procedures.name: Build a natural-base family test
procedures.goal: Expand one slip-based glaze family methodically instead of mixing several romantic branches at once.
steps: Choose one natural base such as Alberta Slip or Ravenscrag Slip.
Mix the plainest base recipe first and test it on the actual body.
After the base passes, choose one branch only: amber, floating blue, celadon, or matte.
If the raw base shrinks badly, add a roasted-branch comparison before changing the chemistry.
Keep every branch on the same body and firing so the family resemblance can be judged clearly.
Photograph the transitions and note whether the family is body-driven, thickness-driven, or additive-driven.
checkpoints: the base was understood before the effect branch was mixed
raw versus roasted handling was documented if relevant
the family is developing from one clear lineage rather than several random recipes
commonMistakes: jumping straight to the dramatic branch and skipping the base
calling a raw-shrinkage problem a chemistry problem
testing the branches on different bodies and then comparing them as if the body did not matter

procedures.name: Promote a glaze to production candidate
procedures.goal: Decide whether a good-looking test result is ready for serious use.
steps: Retest the chosen glaze in a fresh batch instead of trusting the original test bucket.
Apply it on the actual production body and form range, including rims and handles.
Fire it in the normal production schedule with witness cones.
Check fit, run risk, surface quality, cleanability, and whether the visual effect survives the real form.
If the glaze is intended for functional use, keep decorative and functional claims separate until the glaze earns the stronger claim.
Archive the final recipe, batch notes, and photos as the production candidate record.
checkpoints: the second batch reproduced the first result
the glaze was tested on the real production forms
a clear record exists of what exactly is being promoted
commonMistakes: promoting a glaze after one lucky firing
assuming a test tile result transfers cleanly to a mug or bowl interior
forgetting to record the final version after several rounds of edits
